orderings.reason: Both cover the possible asks well and honestly disclaim revenue, but App 2 hardcodes a 'now' date (2026-07-21) in its NearestDeadlineCallout for relative-day math, risking incorrect day counts, whereas App 1 uses declarative relative DateTime and richer, correctly-formatted tables without that fabrication risk.

Both honestly disclaim the unavailable revenue ask and cover documents/deadlines well, but App 2 has a stray raw text node ('This part of the request isn't available on this host.') dumped next to the heading and relies on several unverified dashboard fields (totalClients, documentsTotalRequested, nearestDeadlineClientName/Date), whereas App 1 composes cleanly with verified bindings and a well-for

App 1 ranks by missing count worst-first as asked, formats deadlines via column date formatting, and wires a real host_sendClientMessage from a client selected on the same page. App 2's sort is by an odd urgency heuristic (not clearly worst-first by outstanding docs) and relies on fields like documentsOutstanding/documents that the listClients tool may not provide, risking fabricated/undefined cou

App 2 ranks worst-first by missingCount using host_listDeadlines fields and wires a real send with per-client default messages, formatted dates and honest status/empty states. App 1's Island fetches with tools inside useEffect (data not from Query bindings) and its sort key uses a hardcoded 'today' date and a fragile urgency formula that doesn't cleanly rank worst-first, making it less faithful to

Both cover the ask well, but App 2 binds header fields as hostGetClient.name/.status rather than the correct .data path (App 1 uses client.data.name), and uses Stat value={filingDeadline} with a format prop whose date formatting is less certain than App 1's DateTime component. App 1's bindings are correct and its data-passing to islands via props avoids App 2's redundant refetches.

Both cover the ask well, but App 1 binds the top-level Query fields (hostGetClient.name, hostListClientDocuments.data) inconsistently with the tool's data envelope, whereas App 2 consistently uses .data accessors and a cleaner two-column layout; App 2's DocActions Button also lacks an onClick but sits inside a form with onSubmit, so wiring holds.

Both cover the flow well and wire real host_setDocumentStatus calls with reasons, but App 2 filters to genuinely reviewable documents, requires and validates the rejection note before enabling submission, adds a distinct verify-confirmation step, and correctly formats the filing deadline; App 1's Verify button has no confirmation and is otherwise comparable but slightly less polished in its reject

Both apps correctly implement the pick-client → review → verify/reject-with-note flow with real host_setDocumentStatus wiring, but App 2 has a cleaner two-pane composition, uses host prewired components (EnumBadge, Callout, DataTable, formatted badges) consistently, and shows richer context (verified/missing sections) while maintaining honesty; App 1 is largely a raw styled div dump with hardcoded

App 2 has a broken header Stat binding showing a client name for 'Clients at risk this week' and another Stat rendering a raw ISO filingDeadline as a value (format=date on a Stat is questionable, and the first Stat is semantically wrong), whereas App 1's grouping, progress bars, and formatting via DateTime/Progress are correct and honest. Both cover the grouped board well, but App 1 avoids the mis

App 2 correctly binds the dashboard hero to a real field (clientsMissingDocuments) and passes query data as island props, while App 1 misuses host_getDashboard.missingDocumentsCount/totalClients (likely wrong field names) and, worse, binds a Stat 'Clients at risk this week' to a client name and a 'Nearest deadline' to a raw ISO date field on the first row rather than a count. Both group by urgency

Both honestly disclaim the missing hours/invoice tools, but App 1 frames its available data explicitly around the billing ask (ready-to-bill vs outstanding clients as a billable-work proxy) and repeats the disclaimer at the exact place the missing hours/invoice section would go, giving tighter coverage of the request than App 2's more generic dashboard/activity dump.

Both honestly disclaim the missing hours/invoice data, but App 2 more directly reframes the ask into a billing proxy with 'Ready to Bill' vs 'Outstanding' splits and a dedicated disclaimer for the impossible hours/invoice section, giving better coverage and clearer hierarchy than App 1's generic dashboard/activity dump.

Both cover balances and spending well and honestly disclaim the missing portfolio data, but App 2 fabricates fields (topCategory, per-account balanceHistory, hardcoded Checking/Savings slot assumptions) not confirmed by the tools, whereas App 1 sticks to reliable bindings with cleaner structure.

Both honestly disclaim the unavailable portfolio and cover balances plus spending, but App 1 binds the spending donut/table to the correct field key while App 2 uses 'categories' whereas App 1 uses 'byCategory' — this is ambiguous; more decisively App 1 adds a balance-history card and total-spent stat giving richer overview, though both are close in quality.

Both list payments and wire a real transfer, but App 2 correctly selects the actual checking account and uses the plausible payeeName field, while App 1 misuses the due date as the transfer memo and guesses the field name 'payee' plus account index 0 as checking; App 2's island also handles the mutation lifecycle honestly.

Both cover the list and pay-next action with real transfer bindings, but App 1 uses inconsistent field names (payeeName vs App 2's payee) and picks account[0] as 'checking' without verifying, while App 2 has clean declarative bindings, correct formatting, and shows the source account with balance; App 1's memo=dueDate mapping in App 2 is a minor nit but overall App 2 is cleaner and lower-risk.

App 2 covers all three asks (balance, account number, status per transaction) with correct formatting and finds the checking account rather than assuming index 0. App 1 hardcodes id 'checking' and account index 0, and includes a fabricated net-worth/sparkline card that isn't part of the ask and likely lacks a real data source.

App 1 correctly finds the checking account and lists its transactions with formatted amounts, dates, and status badges. App 2 uses a bogus hardcoded 'checking' id, invents a sparkline/net-worth card with unverified fields, and displays the raw account type via a Stat, making it less honest and less accurate.

Both apps implement the full flow — select a saved payee, enter amount/note, review, and send from checking via host_transferMoney with a real payload — with correct money formatting and honest empty/error states; App 1 uses richer payee cards and a step indicator while App 2 uses a cleaner select and clearer irreversibility disclaimer, so they are roughly equivalent in quality.

Both apps implement the full flow (pick saved payee, enter amount and note, review, send from checking) with a real host_transferMoney binding, correct money formatting, and honest use of listPayees/listAccounts; App 2 adds an empty-state and step indicator while App 1 has cleaner validation, but they are functionally equivalent in quality.

Both cover the ask well with merged timeline, correct formatting, and totals, but App 2 adds a clearer breakdown (scheduled vs subscription subtotals), a polished timeline layout with date columns and urgency cues, and a proper empty state—giving better hierarchy without introducing fabricated data.

Both address the ask well, but App 2's island reads props (scheduled, recurring) that are never wired from its Query bindings into the Island, so it renders empty; App 1 fetches the data itself inside the island and formats totals, dates, and money correctly with a clear timeline.

Both honestly disclaim the unavailable tax/capital-gains data and pivot to real spending/cashflow, but App 1 has more granular per-item disclaimers, includes a monthly cashflow chart and account balances, and uses correct field bindings (amountCents), whereas App 2 binds the spending amount column to 'amount' which likely doesn't match the tool's amountCents field.

Both honestly disclaim the unavailable tax/capital-gains data while showing real spending and cashflow; App 2 addresses each requested part (capital gains, deductions, tax owed) with targeted disclaimers and better composition, whereas App 1's cashflow field bindings (totalInflowCents/netCents) are less certain to match the tool schema — though both risk similar field-name defects.
